channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: America’s Problem With Diplomacy Predates Trump
item.description: Witkoff and Kushner are merely the tip of the iceberg.

item.title: Where Do the Gulf States Go From Here?
item.description: The U.S. war with Iran has shattered their economic model.

item.title: U.S.-Iran Peace Talks May Collapse Before They Even Begin
item.description: Tehran is demanding new conditions for dialogue, as Israel continues its strikes on Lebanon.

item.title: The Iran War’s Agriculture Shock Isn’t Over Yet
item.description: Even with a cease-fire deal in place, vital energy and fertilizer flows remain trapped.

item.title: After the war, how open will the Strait of Hormuz be?
item.description: Access may come at a price

item.title: The Economy Is on the Ballot in Hungary
item.description: How longtime Prime Minister Viktor Orban’s economic record could shape Sunday’s election.

item.title: Cover Story newsletter: Donald Trump is the war’s biggest loser
item.description: An exclusive look at how we designed our cover

item.title: What in the World?
item.description: Test yourself on the week of April 4: Orban prepares for Hungary’s elections, Trump threatens Iran, and Taiwan’s opposition leader visits China.

item.title: Pakistan Walks a Tightrope on Iran
item.description: As Islamabad hosts peace talks, it’s also balancing a security pact with Riyadh.

item.title: The Charisma Wars
item.description: Why personality now trumps policy in global politics.

item.title: Are sugar substitutes healthier than the real thing?
item.description: We share some bitter truths

item.title: The Fight Over the Future of Meat
item.description: The United States lost the EV race to China. Food could be next.

item.title: Meme Wars
item.description: Trump has decimated the norms of diplomatic communication, and other countries have followed suit.

item.title: Hedging Security in the Gulf Is Risky
item.description: The Gulf states have long hedged their diplomatic and security bets, attempting to strike a balance between those that might protect them from threats (especially the US) and those doing the threatening (such as Iran). But this approach has left them highly vulnerable and must be replaced by a unified approach to allies and foes.

item.title: How Iran Hawks Are Viewing the Cease-Fire
item.description: John Bolton explains why he wishes Trump finished the job.

item.title: The Iran War Has Ended a Year of Economic Promise
item.description: Before US President Donald Trump launched his war of choice against Iran, financial markets were booming in many countries, and private-sector confidence was recovering. But the outlook has suddenly become bleaker, and many governments have only limited policy buffers available to cushion the inflationary shock.

item.title: Africa Is Losing the Iran War
item.description: The fallout from the latest war in the Middle East has made visible a problem that many preferred to ignore: the international financial architecture is not fit for a world of cascading shocks, tightening fiscal constraints, and rising human need. Nowhere is this more obvious than in Africa.

item.title: Spain Is the Friend That the United States Needs
item.description: In the Iran war and beyond, Madrid has often done a better job looking out for U.S. interests than Washington.

item.title: Taiwan’s Political Crisis Is a Security Nightmare
item.description: Government paralysis is freezing budgets and wrecking public trust.

item.title: What if the Ottomans Survived?
item.description: Recent scholarship shows the promise—and limits—of a historical path not taken.

item.title: A New Type of Democratic Transition
item.description: When trying to rebuild after illiberal rule, today’s pro-democracy governments discover booby traps laid by their predecessors and confront all kinds of veto players. But Poland’s experience has been instructive, highlighting the need for bold action and the right support from external actors.

item.title: Trump’s Venezuela Report Card
item.description: A look back at the U.S. intervention in Venezuela that the president wants to replicate.

item.title: How to Understand Emerging Risks
item.description: Understanding and anticipating risks is crucial to protecting society from the unpredictable. But the nature of risk is evolving at an unprecedented pace, and the processes and assumptions that guide insurers and their clients in managing uncertainty must be adjusted accordingly.

item.title: Scam Inc has a new weapon
item.description: For the first time, investigators have traced spyware to its physical origin—revealing new types of duplicity

item.title: The Iran War’s Winners and Losers
item.description: The current ceasefire allows us to make a preliminary assessment of the war and its effects. China and Russia were clear winners, while the Gulf Arab states suffered a reputational hit, and the combatants all had decidedly mixed outcomes, although the United States and Israel gained the least.

item.title: No Exit from Stalin
item.description: While Russia has not reverted to full-bore Stalinism, there is no doubt that President Vladimir Putin has made tremendous strides toward totalitarianism. That is why people have sought lessons in history—in particular, in the story of how calls for change emerged at the commanding heights of the USSR.

item.title: Thailand Announces Further Asset Seizures in Widening Scam Probe
item.description: The assets were connected to a network surrounding the South African businessman Benjamin Mauerberger, who is wanted on fraud and money laundering charges.

item.title: This Was the First War Against AI
item.description: The Iran war has revealed the geopolitical miscalculations of the current tech race.

item.title: Europe’s New Defense Core
item.description: As America steps back, four countries will shape the continent’s security.

item.title: Venezuela’s Treacherous Recovery
item.description: The peril and promise of an economic boom in Venezuela.

item.title: The Iran War’s Real Lessons for China
item.description: U.S. tactical successes should give Beijing pause.

item.title: Few Safe Harbors: Migrant Workers Vulnerable in Southeast Asia’s Fishing and Seafood Industries
item.description: Labor conditions aboard the region's poorly regulated fleet of fishing trawlers are a cause for continuing alarm.

item.title: Indonesia’s President Prabowo Subianto Could Visit Russia Soon, Kremlin Says
item.description: The trip could involve discussions about energy purchases from Russia to help shore up Indonesia's domestic fuel supply.

item.title: Friendly Strangers: How Two Peoples Across the Taiwan Strait Are Drifting Apart
item.description: The populations of China and Taiwan are drifting apart in ways that will be structurally hard to reverse.

item.title: India’s Hormuz Dilemma Is Not Yet Over
item.description: New Delhi, which supports UNCLOS, is reportedly uncomfortable with Iran’s plan to levy a toll on every ship that passes the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: China-US Trade Relations: Between Engagement and Decoupling
item.description: Survey findings complicate the notion that U.S.-China economic policy presents a binary choice between the two options.

item.title: China’s Divergent Strategy: Modernizing vs. Stabilizing Energy Infrastructure in Central Asia
item.description: Beijing has not taken a one-size-fits-all approach to its engagement with the energy sectors in Uzbekistan and Kyrgyzstan.

item.title: The Middle Corridor in an Era of Supply Chain Disruption
item.description: The route has proven its value in times of crisis. Can it evolve into a primary artery of Eurasian trade?

item.title: India’s Worrying Plans for Dams on Transboundary Rivers Shared with Bangladesh
item.description: At least seven hydroelectric projects on two transboundary river systems are in the pipeline in the northeast Indian state of Meghalaya.

item.title: The Quiet Quad: From Strategic Signalling to Embedded Minilateralism – or a Silent Drift?
item.description: For a grouping that until recently thrived on high-profile summitry, the Quad’s present silence is striking to say the least.

item.title: The Price of Strategic Autonomy: India and the Iran Conflict
item.description: In a world where conflicts are increasingly interconnected, and expectations of alignment are growing, the space for sustained ambiguity is shrinking.

item.title: Hospitals are stuck in a deadly doom loop
item.description: They never recovered from covid-19

item.title: AI micro-dramas are shaking up Chinese entertainment
item.description: Regulators threaten to spoil the fun
